parties: Between Norman Hector MacPherson, appellant, and Her Majesty the Queen, respondent
court: New Brunswick Court of Appeal Ayles, Ryan and Turnbull JJ.A.
body_html:
Heard: June 6, 1995.
Judgment: June 14, 1995.
(8 pp.)


Appeal from judgment of Desjardins Prov. Ct. J., New Brunswick Provincial Court, April 3, 1995.

Civil rights — Detention and imprisonment — Detention — What constitutes arbitrary detention — Bail and interim release — Right to be taken before a judge within 24 hours — Denial of right — Remedies — Reduction of sentence.

This was an appeal from the conviction of the accused on charges of possession of property obtained by crime and driving while disqualified. The accused was arrested on a Friday and was taken in front of a Provincial Court Judge on the following Monday. He pleaded guilty at that time but also argued that his rights under section 503 of the Criminal Code and section 9 of the Canadian Charter of Rights and Freedoms had been violated in that he was not taken before a Provincial Court Judge as soon as possible after his arrest as required by section 503 of the Code.

HELD: The appeal from conviction was dismissed. The application under section 24(1) of the Charter was allowed. The sentence imposed on the accused for the possession of property conviction was reduced from six months to three months. Even though it was unlikely that the accused would have been released on bail if he had been taken before a judge before the Monday, his right not to be arbitrarily detained under section 9 of the Charter was breached by the failure to bring him before a judge within 24 hours. The delay was caused by the fact that there were no judges available on the weekends in New Brunswick. This systemic defect required that the accused be granted a remedy. Given the considerable criminal record of the accused, it was not appropriate to stay the charges. The appropriate remedy under section 24(1) was to substantially reduce his sentence.

Statutes, Regulations and Rules Cited:

Canadian Charter of Rights and Freedoms, 1982, ss. 9, 24(1). Criminal Code, ss. 259, 354(1), 503, 503(a).

Counsel:

The appellant, on his own behalf.
Jeffrey L. Mockler, for the respondent.

Reasons for judgment by: Ryan J.A. Concurred in by: Ayles J.A. and Turnbull J.A.

RYAN J.A.:— Norman Hector MacPherson was stopped in Florenceville, Judicial District of Woodstock, New Brunswick, driving a car stolen in Nova Scotia. He was en route to Ontario with his girlfriend. He was born September 8, 1963 and has as many criminal convictions as he has years.

MacPherson was arrested on Friday, March 31, 1995, kept in jail over the week-end, and was taken before a Provincial Court judge on Monday, April 3 at Perth-Andover. He pleaded guilty to possession of property obtained by crime, s.354(1), and to driving while disqualified, s.259 of the Criminal Code of Canada. To no avail, he complained to the Provincial Court judge that his rights under s.9 of the Canadian Charter of Rights and Freedoms and under s.503 of the Criminal Code of Canada had been violated. The judge sentenced him to serve a term of imprisonment of six months on the possession charge and two months on the driving charge, the latter to be served concurrently. MacPherson has lodged a prisoner appeal of his convictions claiming that the Provincial Court had no jurisdiction to sentence him because his rights were violated.

The main exchange between the court of first instance and the accused on this point was as follows:

THE COURT: Your big concern a while ago was for Miss Enright.

MR. MACPHERSON: That and a habeas corpus issue of a writ, your honour, that I thought may avail me something because if, I don't know if it should be mentioned here or not, your honour. In other Provinces, per se, they have you in front of a J.P. within twenty-four hours. They guarantee it. They make sure that you do that. I was arrested on a Friday as such. It doesn't happen in this here...

THE COURT: That's right.

MR. MACPHERSON: Province, and I think it's...it's not so much law as it is just unwritten policy that happens around here and well, the judge is not here and we don't have J.P. so it doesn't happen here but as far as the law goes, it would have been possible to have been brought in front of a Justice of the Peace.

THE COURT: In Nova Scotia or Ontario, or in provinces in which they have J.P.'s but...

MR. MACPHERSON: Yeah.

THE COURT: Not in New Brunswick because it says the first...within twenty-four hours or within the first, and Saturdays and Sundays don't count as...in those twenty-four hours because there...

MR. MACPHERSON: Or as soon as possible, it would be possible...

THE COURT: Which is Monday morning.

MR. MACPHERSON: Yeah, that's conveniency.

THE COURT: Well...

MR. MACPHERSON: That's not possibility. That's...I think there's distinction between the two, you know. It's been going on like this for years and it hasn't changed as such in this province as it has in other provinces but I think there is ... there is a ... there is a case there for a habeas corpus if I wish to push it and that was one of my main concern. That's all as far as that goes, your honour.

For whatever reason, the judge failed to deal directly with MacPherson's complaint.

Section 9 of the Charter prohibits arbitrary detention and s.503 of the Criminal Code requires that all arrested persons, if not released by the police, be taken before a Provincial Court judge within 24 hours of arrest or as soon as possible if a judge is not available.

9. Everyone has the right not to be arbitrarily detained or imprisoned.

503. (1) A peace officer who arrests a person with or without warrant or to whom a person is delivered under subsection 494(3) shall cause the person to be detained in custody and, in accordance with the following provisions, to be taken before a justice to be dealt with according to law, namely,

(a) where a justice is available within a period of twenty-four hours after the person has been arrested by or delivered to the peace officer, the person shall be taken before a justice without unreasonable delay and in any event within that period, and

(b) where a justice is not available within a period of twenty-four hours after the person has been arrested by or delivered to the peace officer, the person shall be taken before a justice as soon as possible.

unless, at any time before the expiration of the time prescribed in paragraph (a) or (b) for taking the person before a justice,

(c) the peace officer or officer in charge releases the person under any other provision of this Part, or

(d) the peace officer or officer in charge is satisfied that the person should be released from custody, whether unconditionally under sub-section (4) or otherwise conditionally or unconditionally, and so releases him.

Section 503(a) requires that, in New Brunswick, every person under arrest be taken before a provincial court judge without unreasonable delay, and in any event within 24 hours. The 24 hour time frame in sub-clause (a) is being construed strictly by the courts. One of the purposes of a strict interpretation is so that persons will not languish in jail on weekends. For example, we were informed that at Woodstock where MacPherson would ordinarily have appeared in court, a provincial court judge is unavailable between Friday of one week and Tuesday of the following week. For this reason, MacPherson was taken to Perth-Andover on the Monday following his arrest. This situation is not unique to Woodstock but applies in other areas of the Province.

There are no justices of the peace in New Brunswick and only 26 full time judges of a busy Provincial Court. The province has no provision for justices of the peace, an archaic system which was discontinued in the 1960's, but undoubtedly serves some useful purpose in other provinces with different population and geographic centers than New Brunswick. The system of deputy provincial court judges was also discontinued for reasons of substance. In addition, the Province recently discontinued the supernumerary status for judges who have attained 65 years of age.

In any event, there are areas in the Province which are not serviced on weekends by the Provincial Courts, a systemic defect.

A person under arrest is supposed to be taken before a judge without unreasonable delay, and in any event within 24 hours. When an accused is not, his or her continued detention becomes arbitrary and, therefore, a violation of s.9 of the Charter which prohibits arbitrary detention.

The Supreme Court of Canada in R. v. Simpson, [1995] 1 S.C.R. 449 in a terse judgment upheld the trial judge and reversed a majority decision of the Newfoundland Court of Appeal. In that case the 21 year old accused had been arrested on charges of assault causing bodily harm and of public mischief in making a false allegation of assault. The accused was arrested on Sunday afternoon. Monday was a holiday and so the accused was not taken before a judge until Tuesday. There was no system for judges to be available on weekends for the purposes of releasing detainees on condition. The police objected to the release of the accused unless she was released on condition that she stay away from certain persons. On Tuesday she was released upon this condition.

Subsequently, the trial judge held that her right, under s.9 of the Charter, not to be arbitrarily detained, had been violated. He entered a stay of the proceedings. In a brief decision, the majority of the Newfoundland Court of Appeal set aside the stay. The majority held that her remedy under s.24(1) of the Charter was to sue civilly. Mr. Justice O'Neill, in dissent, held otherwise. He said in (1994) 29 C.R. (4th) 274 at 275, and I cite the entire judgment in dissent:

There had been a clear and serious breach of s.9 and it could not be said that the trial judge was in error in exercising his discretion under s.24(1) of the Charter to stay the proceedings. He had thoroughly canvassed various other remedies, such as reduction of sentence and damages, and concluded that no remedy short of a stay would be appropriate and just. Appropriate civil damages would require an application in the Supreme Court with increased legal fees, delay and inconvenience. An arbitrary deprivation of liberty is not acceptable and affording no remedy would condone the deprivation of liberty.

In an annotation to the Simpson case, Prof. Don Stuart, Editor-in-Chief of the Criminal Reports said at page 276:

Under the Charter, it is trite that issues do arise in the criminal trial independent of guilt. The important remedy of exclusion of evidence under s.24(2)has often resulted in the acquittal of the guilty. Furthermore, the Supreme Court in R. v. Strachan, [1988] 2 S.C.R. 980, 67 C.R. (3d) 878, [1989] 1 W.W.R. 385, 90 N.R. 273, 46 C.C.C. (3d) 479, 56 D.L.R. (4th) 673, 37 C.R.R. 335, has in that context rejected a strict causal test, even though s.24(2) speaks of a possibility of exclusion of evidence "obtained in a manner that infringed or denied: any Charter right. In the case of a breach of such important protections as that against arbitrary detention, the courts should be receptive to a meaningful remedy under the open-ended s. 24(1) such as a stay or, in some cases, a reduction in sentence. It is disingenuous to rely on the availability of a civil remedy for damages given the difficulty and cost of bringing such actions against authorities and the sparse record of success both before and after the Charter.

Section 24(1) of the Charter is as follows:

24. (1) Anyone whose rights or freedoms, as guaranteed by this Charter, have been infringed or denied may apply to a court of competent jurisdiction to obtain such remedy as the court considers appropriate and just in the circumstances.

The Supreme Court of Canada heard the appeal as of right, and in the vast majority of appeals now heard by it "as of right", handed down its decision orally. In a two sentence judgment the five judge panel held:

LAMER C.J. - With respect, we are all of the view that there was no reason for interfering with the exercise of discretion by the trial judge. The appeal is therefore allowed and the stay entered by the trial judge is restored.

In New Brunswick, as was the case in Newfoundland, there is no system in place for judges to be available on weekends. This is a systemic defect which, until now, has resulted in arrested persons wrongly being detained in violation of s.9 of the Charter when they are not taken before a judge as required under s. 503 of the Criminal Code. Since it is a systemic defect in the judicial district, it does not, as perhaps the judge of first instance thought, trigger a discussion of s.503(1)(b) "where a justice is not available within a period of twenty-four hours after the person has been arrested. . .". Justice does not stop on weekends.

In R. v. Charles (1987), 59 C.R. (3d) 94 the Saskatchewan Court of Appeal upheld the substantial reduction of a sentence which normally would have been imposed on an assault charge. They declined to quash the conviction. However, under the recent Simpson decision, a stay also would have been considered appropriate.

Here, MacPherson has a lengthy record going from coast to coast. He told us that there is an outstanding warrant for his arrest for failing to appear in a court in British Columbia. At the time of his arrest in Florenceville he was passing through New Brunswick in a stolen car. His chances of being released, even if a judge had been available, were, to put it in perspective, negligible. Be that as it may, his rights to be taken before a judge within 24 hours and not to be arbitrarily detained, were violated. Under the circumstances, and to emphasize that a violation of this Charter right is serious, I would dismiss his appeal against convictions but allow his application under s.24(1) of the Charter of Rights and Freedoms. I would substantially reduce his sentence by setting aside the sentence of six months under s. 354(1) and impose a sentence of three months. I would not disturb the sentence of two months imposed under s.259 of the Criminal Code.

As noted by Prof. Stuart in his annotation, Lamer J. (as he then was) in R. v. Mills, [1986] 1 S.C.R 863 recognized that the power of a court of competent jurisdiction to give "appropriate and just" remedies under s. 24(1) for Charter violations establishes the:

. . . right to a remedy as the foundation stone for the effective enforcement of Charter rights.

See also the decision of this court in R. v. Dennison (1991), 109 N.B.R.(2d) 388, application for leave to appeal refused by the Supreme Court of Canada (1991), 111 N.B.R.(2d)90, where the sentence was reduced under s.24(1) because of the refusal of the sentencing judge to permit the accused to speak to sentence.

RYAN J.A.
We concur:

AYLES J.A.
TURNBULL J.A.
